title: Matelea tristiflora
intro: Matelea tristiflora, also known as "black swallow-wort," is a perennial plant native to North America, belonging to the Apocynaceae family. The plant is valued for its potential health benefits, which include anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antioxidant properties, making it a sought-after remedy for various health issues. Bioactive constituents of Matelea tristiflora include flavonoids, alkaloids, and terpenoids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. Matelea tristiflora is often used to prepare herbal teas, tinctures, and infusions, which are used to treat conditions such as arthritis, wounds, and skin infections.
taxonomy: Matelea tristiflora is classified within the family Apocynaceae, which is a large and diverse group of flowering plants commonly known as the dogbane or oleander family. This family includes over 5,000 species of plants, many of which are native to tropical and subtropical regions. Matelea tristiflora is a species of flowering plant native to South America, and its classification reflects its unique characteristics and evolutionary history. The Apocynaceae family is further divided into several subfamilies, including the Apocynoideae subfamily to which Matelea tristiflora belongs. Matelea tristiflora is also classified as part of the genus Matelea, which comprises over 120 species of plants.
uses: Matelea tristiflora is a plant species known for its potential medicinal properties. Research on Matelea tristiflora has shown that it contains compounds with anti-inflammatory and antioxidant activities.
